refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall update the worksite model to reflect current equipment state, environmental conditions, and operational events with a p95 latency not exceeding the customer-defined target (baseline 1.0 s) across all supported update scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-007 requires explicit binding to measurable latency acceptance criteria and clarification of what constitutes 'worksite model' (equipment state, environment, events). The refinement anchors the requirement to the p95 metric and customer-defined targets, enabling testable verification. High safety criticality demands that latency bounds protect operator situational awareness and safety decision-making.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency from sensor/event generation through worksite model update completion across representative scenarios (equipment movement, obstacle detection, tele-operator command feedback, safety event propagation). Validate p95 latency against acceptance threshold under nominal and degraded network conditions.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-007.1: Acquire real-time equipment state (position, orientation, operational mode) with sufficient frequency and accuracy to support worksite model updates.
FR-NGHE-007.2: Transmit equipment state and event data to the worksite model service with bounded network latency.
FR-NGHE-007.3: Process and integrate incoming state/event data into the worksite model representation within latency budget.
FR-NGHE-007.4: Distribute updated worksite model to authorized consumers (operator displays, tele-operator interface, safety services) with minimal additional latency.
FR-NGHE-007.5: Monitor and report worksite model update latency; alert if p95 latency exceeds customer-defined threshold.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Centralized worksite model service with synchronous state ingestion and push-based distribution to consumers; latency managed via in-memory caching and optimized serialization.
DP-B: Distributed edge-based model replicas on vehicle and tele-operator platforms with asynchronous eventual-consistency synchronization; latency optimized by local model access.

DP-C: Hybrid architecture: vehicle maintains local equipment state cache; central worksite model service aggregates and distributes via publish-subscribe with configurable QoS; latency tuned per consumer priority.
DP-D: Real-time streaming pipeline (Kafka/RabbitMQ) with stateless processors; worksite model materialized as queryable view; consumers subscribe to model change events.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid architecture with vehicle-local state cache, central worksite model aggregation service, and publish-subscribe distribution with configurable QoS and priority-based latency tuning.

potential_sources: Vehicle state acquisition (FR-NGHE-007.1) couples to vehicle telemetry interface; decoupled via standardized Telemetry interface contract.
Network transmission (FR-NGHE-007.2) couples to network infrastructure; decoupled via Network Link interface abstraction.
Worksite model processing (FR-NGHE-007.3) couples to safety services for event prioritization; decoupled via SafetyAlert interface.
Distribution to consumers (FR-NGHE-007.4) couples to operator display and tele-operator UI; decoupled via Tasking and Telemetry interfaces.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C exhibits low coupling because it isolates concerns: vehicle owns local state, central service owns aggregation logic, publish-subscribe decouples producers from consumers. Interface contracts (Telemetry, Network Link, SafetyAlert, Tasking) provide clear boundaries.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Vehicle can reliably acquire and cache equipment state at sufficient frequency (≥10 Hz) to support 1.0 s p95 latency target.
Assumption: Network Link provides bounded latency for state transmission; if network is highly variable, p95 target may be unachievable without edge processing.

Risk: Hybrid architecture introduces eventual-consistency windows; if vehicle cache and central model diverge, safety-critical decisions may be made on stale data. Mitigation: implement consistency monitoring and alert thresholds.
Risk: Publish-subscribe QoS configuration complexity; misconfiguration could cause latency violations for low-priority consumers. Mitigation: define QoS profiles and validate per consumer class.
Risk: Central worksite model service becomes a bottleneck if ingestion rate exceeds processing capacity. Mitigation: implement backpressure and load shedding policies.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly specify: (1) vehicle state cache update frequency and retention policy, (2) central service ingestion and processing SLA (e.g., 95th percentile latency budget allocation), (3) publish-subscribe QoS profiles per consumer type (operator display, tele-operator, safety services), (4) consistency monitoring and alert thresholds, (5) network latency assumptions and fallback strategies.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: End-to-end worksite model update pipeline: vehicle state acquisition and caching, central worksite model aggregation and distribution service, network transmission, and consumer delivery. Includes orchestration of Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules via standardized interfaces.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle → NGHE Solution: equipment state (position, orientation, mode, sensor readings). NGHE Solution → Vehicle: model updates and state confirmation. Supports bidirectional state synchronization.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution ↔ Tele-operator platform: state transmission and command feedback. Provides bounded-latency transport; latency characteristics critical to p95 target.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services → NGHE Solution: safety events and constraints. NGHE Solution → Safety Services: worksite model updates relevant to safety decisions. Enables safety-aware latency prioritization.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Tele-operator → NGHE Solution: operational commands and model queries. NGHE Solution → Tele-operator: updated worksite model and command acknowledgments. Supports tele-operator situational awareness.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural container for end-to-end worksite model update orchestration. Owns central aggregation logic, distribution service, and latency management. Minimal diffusion: only external interfaces (Telemetry, Network Link, SafetyAlert, Tasking) cross boundaries.

Vehicle owns equipment state acquisition and local caching (FR-NGHE-007.1), but does not own central aggregation, distribution, or latency management. If worksite model update logic were distributed to Vehicle, it would require Vehicle to understand global model semantics and coordinate with other vehicles—high diffusion and coupling.

Teleoperation owns tele-operator interface and command/feedback loop, but not worksite model aggregation or distribution to other consumers (e.g., operator display, safety services). Assigning worksite model update to Teleoperation would create tight coupling and diffusion across consumer-specific logic.

Safety Services owns safety event detection and constraint enforcement, but not worksite model aggregation or distribution. Assigning worksite model update to Safety Services would conflate safety logic with data pipeline orchestration, reducing modularity and reusability.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the appropriate owner because it: (1) owns the end-to-end latency budget and p95 SLA, (2) orchestrates interactions between Vehicle (state source), Teleoperation (consumer), and Safety Services (event prioritization), (3) centralizes worksite model aggregation and distribution logic, (4) manages network transmission and QoS, (5) minimizes diffusion by keeping model update logic in one place rather than scattering it across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution becomes a monolithic service if worksite model update logic is not further decomposed into sub-modules (e.g., state ingestion, aggregation, distribution). Mitigation: plan next-level decomposition to isolate concerns.

Risk: Central worksite model service is a single point of failure; if it becomes unavailable, all consumers lose model updates. Mitigation: implement redundancy and failover strategies at next decomposition level.

Risk: Latency SLA is difficult to achieve if network latency is highly variable or if central service is overloaded. Mitigation: implement monitoring, alerting, and load shedding; validate assumptions about network and processing capacity.

Risk: Interface contracts (Telemetry, Network Link, SafetyAlert, Tasking) must be precisely defined to avoid latency violations due to serialization, protocol overhead, or synchronization delays. Mitigation: specify interface latency budgets and validate in integration testing.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that NGHE Solution module has sufficient architectural authority and resource allocation to own end-to-end latency SLA. Define internal sub-module boundaries (state ingestion, aggregation, distribution) and interface contracts with explicit latency budgets. Establish monitoring and alerting for p95 latency violations. Plan for redundancy and failover strategies.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid architecture with vehicle-local cache, central aggregation, and publish-subscribe distribution) directly addresses FR-NGHE-007 (worksite model update latency ≤ 1.0 s p95). DP-C enables latency optimization through edge caching (reduces network round-trip), central aggregation (enables consistent model), and QoS-based distribution (prioritizes safety-critical consumers). Coupling is low; interfaces are well-defined. Assumptions and risks are documented and mitigatable.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk: If worksite model update logic is not cleanly separated from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules, latency management becomes distributed and difficult to control. Mitigation: NGHE Solution owns orchestration; Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services interact via standardized interfaces.

Diffusion risk: Network Link interface latency characteristics are outside NGHE Solution's direct control; if network is unreliable, p95 target may be unachievable. Mitigation: define network latency assumptions and fallback strategies; monitor and alert on violations.

Diffusion risk: SafetyAlert interface requires coordination between NGHE Solution and Safety Services for event prioritization; if not carefully designed, could introduce latency coupling. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert contract with explicit latency budgets and asynchronous semantics.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate container for worksite model update orchestration. Containment score of 95 reflects that all core logic (state ingestion, aggregation, distribution) resides within NGHE Solution. Diffusion score of 5 reflects minimal external dependencies (only interface contracts). Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are consumers/producers, not owners of worksite model update logic.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: (1) instrument Vehicle, NGHE Solution, and consumer interfaces to measure end-to-end latency, (2) run representative scenarios (equipment movement, obstacle detection, tele-operator feedback, safety events), (3) collect latency samples and compute p95 percentile, (4) validate against customer-defined target (baseline 1.0 s). Verification can be performed in lab and field environments.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns worksite model update latency SLA. Vehicle owns state acquisition frequency and accuracy. Network Link owns transmission latency. Safety Services owns event prioritization. Teleoperation owns consumer interface latency. Each module has a clear responsibility; latency budget can be allocated across modules and validated.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-C to specify latency budget allocation: e.g., Vehicle state acquisition (100 ms), network transmission (200 ms), central service processing (400 ms), distribution to consumers (300 ms). Total: 1000 ms p95.
Define interface contracts (Telemetry, Network Link, SafetyAlert, Tasking) with explicit latency SLAs and QoS profiles.
Plan next-level decomposition of NGHE Solution to isolate state ingestion, aggregation, and distribution sub-modules.
Establish monitoring and alerting for p95 latency violations; define escalation procedures.
Validate network latency assumptions; define fallback strategies if network is unreliable.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-007 is not atomic; it encompasses multiple concerns (state acquisition, transmission, aggregation, distribution, monitoring). DP-C is sound and well-coupled, but requires further decomposition to allocate latency budgets and define sub-module responsibilities. IM assignment to NGHE Solution is appropriate, but NGHE Solution itself must be decomposed into sub-modules (state ingestion, aggregation, distribution) to manage complexity and enable independent verification. Proceeding to next decomposition level is necessary to refine latency allocation, define interface contracts, and establish ownership of sub-concerns.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-007.1: Acquire real-time equipment state (position, orientation, operational mode) with sufficient frequency and accuracy to support worksite model updates.
FR-NGHE-007.2: Transmit equipment state and event data to the worksite model service with bounded network latency.
FR-NGHE-007.3: Process and integrate incoming state/event data into the worksite model representation within latency budget.
FR-NGHE-007.4: Distribute updated worksite model to authorized consumers (operator displays, tele-operator interface, safety services) with minimal additional latency.
FR-NGHE-007.5: Monitor and report worksite model update latency; alert if p95 latency exceeds customer-defined threshold.

assumptions: Vehicle can reliably acquire and cache equipment state at ≥10 Hz frequency.
Network Link provides bounded latency for state transmission; network latency is predictable and within acceptable range (e.g., <200 ms p95).
Central worksite model service can process and aggregate state updates within latency budget (e.g., <400 ms p95).
Publish-subscribe distribution can deliver model updates to consumers within latency budget (e.g., <300 ms p95).
Safety Services can process SafetyAlert events asynchronously without blocking worksite model updates.
Tele-operator and operator display can consume model updates at the rate they are produced without backpressure.
open_risks: Network latency variability: if network is highly variable or congested, p95 target may be unachievable. Mitigation: implement network monitoring and fallback strategies.
Central service bottleneck: if ingestion rate exceeds processing capacity, latency will degrade. Mitigation: implement load shedding and backpressure policies.
Eventual consistency: vehicle cache and central model may diverge; safety-critical decisions may be made on stale data. Mitigation: implement consistency monitoring and alert thresholds.
Interface latency overhead: serialization, protocol overhead, and synchronization delays may consume significant portion of latency budget. Mitigation: profile and optimize interface implementations.
Monitoring and alerting complexity: detecting p95 latency violations requires sophisticated instrumentation and analysis. Mitigation: implement automated monitoring and alerting.
evidence_needed: Network latency characterization: measure p95, p99 latency under nominal and degraded conditions.
Vehicle state acquisition frequency and accuracy: validate that Vehicle can provide state at ≥10 Hz with acceptable accuracy.
Central service processing capacity: measure throughput and latency under various load conditions.
Interface latency overhead: profile serialization, protocol, and synchronization delays for each interface.
End-to-end latency validation: measure p95 latency across representative scenarios in lab and field environments.
Safety impact analysis: assess risk of eventual consistency windows on safety-critical decisions.
